speaker: interviewer
text: OK. My name is Beth Wainwright. I'm interviewing Dr. Sol Messinger. It's May 15, 1996. We're in Buffalo, New York. I'm conducting the interview in English. And we are in Buffalo, New York, United States of America.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Beth Wainwright. And I'm interviewing Dr. Sol Messinger. And the date is May 15, 1996. The spelling of my name is W-A-I-N-W-R-I-G-H-T. Dr. Messinger, would you please tell us your name and your name at birth and spell it for us and the other information.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sol Messinger, M-E-S-S-I-N-G-E-R. My name at birth was Salo, S-A-L-O. And during my various travels, I had other names. My-- in France, my name was Solomon, and my Hebrew name is Shlomo. And the only nickname I have is the one that my mother gives-- gave me, and that's [? Schleimaale. ?] I was born in-- on June 16th, 1932 in Berlin, Germany. And I'm 63 years old now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your name for us, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. M-E-S-S-I-N-G-E-R is my-- is the spelling of my last name. My first name is S-O-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Would you please begin by telling us about your childhood as far as you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, as I just mentioned, I was born in June of 1932 in Germany. My parents were from Poland. My father had come to Berlin in 1928. Conditions, economic conditions in Poland were not good and there was a lot of anti-Semitism. My mother at that time went to Vienna to be with her grandparents. She came to Berlin in 1931. They got married, and I was born a year later. Parenthetically, I-- my father and my mother's whole families were in Poland and remained there, or at least most of them. Anyway, June of '32 I guess was not the greatest time to be born in Berlin, because Hitler came to power in January of 1933. And as soon as that happened, things began to go badly for-- for Jews. He had made no secret of the fact-- of how he felt about Jews. The Nuremberg laws were passed. It became difficult to work in Germany. My father was a tailor, so-- and he worked for his-- his older brother Alter. So he continued to work. But it was difficult for professionals to work and so on. And there were other problems because of the law. Of course, you know, I was a little kid. I was not-- not aware of any of this. I guess the only thing that I was aware of was that my parents were more nervous than-- than they had been. And I guess this communicated itself to me. There were some things, though, that I do remember, maybe when I was four years old. I remember that we used to go to the park a lot. And there came a time when they painted certain park benches yellow and those benches were reserved for Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had a choice. You could sit on the benches and then people would come by, they would know that you're Jewish and make disparaging comments or spit on you or whatever. Or you could not sit. You could-- you could just not sit anywhere. Or you could pretend that you were not Jewish and sit in one of the Aryan benches. But of course, then you were taking a chance, because you could be arrested or worse if you were caught. So it was-- you know, it was unpleasant, because I was used to just going to the park. And I realized right away that there was something wrong when my parents said, no, you can't sit there. You must sit here, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember also [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was walking on the street once. I don't know how old I was, maybe five. And some kids said-- I don't know. They said, you're Jewish and they started beating me up. And I ran away. I ran home. I-- I wasn't really hurt very much, but it-- certainly psychologically it was a big trauma. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And later, I suppose when I was maybe six, probably around 1937, '38, I remember I was standing at our window. We lived in an apartment building in Berlin. And I was standing there looking down. We were I think on the fourth floor. I was looking down. Now, a crowd gathered. Now, across the street, actually we were in a Jewish neighborhood. And across the street, there were quite a few Jewish stores. A crowd had accumulated. And then I saw that somebody in the crowd was-- broke the windows. I guess they had some rocks. It was a shoe store. And they just went in and they emptied the store. You could see them just taking out the shoes. And what I-- what I can't forget is that there were policemen who were right there. They were standing in back of the crowd and they were just watching this and doing absolutely nothing to prevent it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Soon after that, I remember going to synagogue with my father on Saturday morning. We used to walk to synagogue. It's one of the nice memories I have of my father. We walked together to synagogue. And I remember we were walking on some of the streets in the neighborhood. And again I saw a lot of stores with broken windows. And of course, they were Jewish stores which had been broken into. All of this-- you know, and I-- I understood in one way or another as a young kid that this was aimed at Jews and I was Jewish and so it was aimed at me. And it was very scary. I was-- I was-- you know, I was upset about this. I was scared. I was afraid to go on the street by myself certainly, but even with my parents I was afraid. So it was-- it was not pleasant. On the other hand, there were some nice things too. I mean, I remember the whole family went on picnics. My-- my father's brother lived in Berlin, he and his wife and two daughters. And his two sisters also lived in Berlin, Rose and Frieda. Frieda was married to Alan. She had a daughter Edith. And the-- my three cousins, the three girls, we were about-- you know, pretty close in age, so we were really good friends. Used to go on picnics. And one summer we actually went to the North Sea. And I-- I don't remember actually swimming, but I remember being on a boat with my parents and my uncle. And actually, we have a picture of that. I suppose that's why I remember it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know, there were-- there were some nice things that happened also. But I don't remember. I remember the bad things more, I suppose because it had an emotional or more of an emotional impact on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on-- as I told you, my father was of Polish origin. On January-- I'm sorry. On October 28, the night of October 28th 1938, we were sleeping, my parents and I. And there was a loud knock on the door. And my-- my parents had a pretty good idea what that meant. A load knock in the middle of the night meant the Gestapo, the secret police of Germany. And they opened the door and indeed it was two Gestapo men. And they came into the apartment and told my father that-- to get dressed. He had to-- they were taking him away.

speaker: interviewer
text: My mother was, you can imagine, panicked. She said, where are you taking him? And they said something like shut up, it's none of your business.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I, of course, started screaming. I was scared out of my mind. And I remember the Gestapo-- one of the Gestapo guys said to my mother, if you don't shut that kid up, I'm going to kill him. So my mother came and she-- she tried to comfort me. And she-- she put her-- she actually put her hand over my mouth. Anyway, they took my father away. My mother had no idea where or why or anything. The next morning, she took me and we went to the police station. Now, of course, going to the police station at that time in Germany-- this was the end of 1938-- was not something that you liked-- a Jewish woman liked to do. But she did. In any case, when we got there, there was a whole crowd of people, women and children. And it turned out that the Germans, the Gestapo had taken all [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish men of Polish citizenship, had picked them all up and had shipped them back to Poland. So my father and one of my uncles was shipped back. The uncle that owned the tailor shop had already left. They were lucky. I guess he made an application for a visa to the United States early, and they had left for the United States, he, his wife, and my two cousins, a month before this all happened. So he-- he was not deported. He was-- they were already in the States. Well, here-- here was my mother. She was 33 at the time, left in Germany without any visible means of support with a young child. I was five. And you can imagine how terrible that was. And at first, she didn't really know what happened to my father, because they just-- the word was out that they had sent them back. But I guess a week later she received a letter from my father. He had gotten to Poland. And-- and actually, he had gone back to the little town where my grandparents lived, his parents. It was a little town called Czermin. It was really-- that was a village. The little town nearby was Mielic. And I guess he stayed there for a few days. And then he went to Krakow, which was the larger city nearby, where his-- one of his married sisters, Ida, lived. And he got a job there as a tailor. So-- so there-- there we were, my mother and I in Germany alone. It was terrible for my mother. And it was terrible for me, because you know, after all these little experiences and this big experience, I was really very scared. The-- this deportation of Polish Jewish men from Germany had another side effect, because what happened was there was a young guy living in Paris by the name of Grynszpan whose father had also been deported from Germany back to Poland. He was infuriated by this. And he decided that he was going to do something. And the something that he was going to do was try to assassinate the-- the German ambassador to Paris. And I guess he did attempt it. I believe that he just wounded one of the attaches. Nobody really got hurt. But this served as the pretext for Kristallnacht, which occurred a little over a week later. My father was deported on October 28 and Kristallnacht was the night of November 9th-10th. I-- I don't remember Kristallnacht itself. I mean, I guess I was sleeping. I certainly wasn't outside at the time. But I do remember that I couldn't go to school for a couple of days. And then finally, my mother got word that the school was open again. Now, you-- you have to understand that by that time Jewish children could not go to German schools. They could only go to-- to Jewish schools, so I was going to a Jewish school. The school that I was at was right next to a synagogue. As a matter of fact, there was sort of a courtyard. The synagogue was in the back of the courtyard. The school was on one side. And in front of this courtyard, separating the courtyard from the street, was the school gym. Well, when my mother took me back to school after Kristallnacht, we came to the school and I saw all the windows of the gym were broken. Apparently that's how they had gotten into the courtyard to get to the synagogue. And then when we walked into the courtyard, I could see that the synagogue had been burned down. I could not only see it, but I could smell it. You could still smell the smoldering smoke. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I can-- it sounds funny, but I can still sort of smell that smell. It's something that you just can't forget. And of course, it-- I don't know if you can imagine even what it felt like to know that the synagogue that you had been attending with your parents-- I told you I used to go with my father on Saturday mornings-- was burned down. It was burned down because people didn't like Jews, namely me. I mean, it was-- it was just a terrible, terrible [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] feeling. You really felt violated. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But, you know, one-- one-- in spite of all these things happening, one tries to carry on as normally as one can. I continued to go to school until January of 1939, when I got diphtheria. So it wasn't enough that my mother-- my father was in Poland. My mother was left alone without any support with a little child, but this little child developed diphtheria, which in those days was a-- was a-- could be a fatal disease. There were no antibiotics. I guess I was treated with an antiserum. And I-- I obviously survived. But it was just another terrible business in this terrible sequence of events. I told you that my uncle Alter had already emigrated to the United States in September of 1938. It became pretty clear to most Jews living in Germany that, you know, it was an untenable situation, that you just couldn't stay there. And so a lot of people [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] looked for countries to go to. We had some relatives in the United States. And besides, the United States had a reputation of-- of-- of being a good place to live in, and so a lot of people applied for a visa to the United States, including my father. I don't know exactly when he applied. It was probably early 1935 or something like that. Now, you know, the United States used to allow immigrants to come in freely. I mean, there were no immigration laws. The only thing was that if you were sick or you had certain diseases, you were not allowed in. You were sent back. But that-- that encompassed about maybe 1% of the people who actually arrived on the shores of the United States. But in 1924, an immigration bill was passed, which severely limited the number of immigrants who come in-- who could come to the United States. It was based on a quota system. And as I understand it, the quota depended on first of all where you were born and the number or the percentage of people from that country who already lived in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my father of course was in the Polish-- in the Polish quota. And it was not a large quota. But there were a tremendous number of applicants. And of course, too many applicants to get visas in a particular year. So what the immigration office in the United States apparently did was they issued you numbers. In other words, if they couldn't fill your-- give you a visa that year, they said, OK. We know that the next year we're going to have certain-- a certain number of visas for Polish citizens, of which you are one. And then the following year, there's another certain number. And we'll give you a number. Whenever that number comes up, then you can come to the consulate. We'll let you know. And you can get your visa to the United States. So we had-- we had that number. And we knew that eventually we would come to the United States. But you know, nobody-- we couldn't wait. I mean, things were terrible in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Going back to Poland was-- was not a solution, because my parents had left Poland for reasons that they-- they didn't want to come back or go back there. The European countries really had very, very, very strict immigration laws. And so the other countries of Europe were not a-- a solution either. There was Palestine, but there were problems getting to Palestine too. And of course, the British-- this was-- I'm not sure exactly what year it was but either 1938 or 1939-- really severely restricted Jewish immigration to Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we looked for-- for someplace else to go to until we could get a visa to the United States, anyplace, we-- you know, as long as we were out of Germany. And I guess my father tried many places. And so did everybody else who was living in Germany, that is, Jews. And some people actually-- I'm sure you know-- went to China, to Shanghai. There was quite a large Jewish, German-Jewish community in Shanghai at the beginning of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, my aunts and my father heard that you could get-- you could get visas to go into Cuba. And so they applied for a Cuban visa. My one aunt, Frieda her husband Alan, and their daughter Edith left Germany, I believe it was in December of 1938, for Cuba. And they arrived in Cuba OK. And my other aunt, Rose, left-- I remember her leaving as a matter of fact, because I was sick with diphtheria. I-- actually I was recuperating already. And you know, she was-- besides my mother, she was the last relative there. And she was leaving. And I remember screaming and asking not to-- or yelling not to-- at her not to leave. She left and she got to Cuba OK. She went to Cuba with her fiance, Louie Meyer, who-- who had lived next door to us. My aunt Rose had lived with us for a period of time, so they got to know each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I ask you how your mother was providing for you during this time? You know, I really don't know. Don't remember.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was working in Poland. And I think that he was sending some money to her. I think also that we did have some relatives in the United States. It's possible that they sent some money. I-- I'm not sure about that. And it's also possible that the Jewish community in Germany or in other countries provided some money to these women who had been left alone when their husbands were deported. But I'm not sure. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how it was at your house? Was there food for you to eat? Was there heat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- I don't remember being cold and I don't remember not eating except when I was sick. I mean, you know, I had no appetite. I guess there was food. I think-- also, I think my mother started selling off stuff that we had, not that we had a lot. But what she had, she started selling, so there was some-- she did manage to get some money. Well, as I was saying, you know, we had made these applications for a visa to Cuba. My father had done all this work, but he was deported. So it fell to my mother to pursue all of this. And part of it involved going to these German officers, immigration officers, God knows what other officers. Well, you know, for a Jewish woman to go into these offices was at least unpleasant, if not dangerous. And I'm sure my mother-- she never really talked about this, but I'm sure she didn't want to go. But-- but there was no choice. I mean, either she did it or we-- we-- we wouldn't get a visa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she-- I remember she-- she told me she was at this office, she was at that office. And-- and I remember that she would come home upset. You know, I really-- when I think about it now, my mother is a very mild-mannered, lovely, nice lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: How she managed to do all this, to go through all of this without my father, with me sick, going to all these offices, I-- I don't know. I guess-- I guess when you have to do something, you just do it. But it-- it amazes me that- that she was able to do all of this. In any case, in I guess it must have been March or April of 1939, we got word that we had a visa to Cuba. And then my mother had to arrange for passage. And she arranged that. She got passage for the three of us on the ship called the And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yeah. We had to pay for that. I-- I have no idea how she did it. Maybe we had some savings. I-- I really don't know, but she did. But then the problem was that my father was in Poland. And we were-- the ship was leaving from Hamburg, so he needed to come to Germany in order for us to leave together. And what he needed was a transit visa, in other words, a visa saying that he could come in to Germany for three days or two days or whatever so that he could leave from Hamburg. And my mother, again she had to go to the offices and try to get this and so on. And my father, I guess he had to go to the German embassy or legation in Krakow to-- to get these papers. Now, we-- we had passage on the ship for May 14th, 1939. And we-- my mother had sold what she could. I mean, you really couldn't sell very much because the Germans knew that there was no way that we could take this stuff along with us, the furniture and so on, so they-- they paid peanuts for it-- for it. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so she sold some of the stuff off. My father, we didn't know whether he was going to be able to come. But he had written my mother. He had said, if I don't come, I want you to take your child, our child and go on the ship. I mean, it's too dangerous for you to be in-- in Germany. I'll find some way to join you eventually. It was the day before we were supposed to leave Berlin for Hamburg, where we were going to embark on the ship. And my mother was beside herself. Whatever needed packing was packed. We were ready to go. And my father wasn't there. And she was-- I remember the scene so well. She was talking to her neighbor, the eventual mother-in-law of my Aunt Rose. And suddenly, there was this knock on the door. [LAUGHS] And my mother screamed because she recognized the knock. It was my father. And I remember, you know, we hadn't seen him for about seven months. And I just remember that reunion very well. And so that part of the story had a very good outcome. We got on the train. We got to Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we interrupt you here? We have to break tape.
